blocks.content: The small file problem is one of the most common performance killers at scale. A streaming pipeline writing every minute creates 1440 files/day per partition. After one year: 500,000+ files in a single directory.

rows: Each file = one NameNode inode. 150 bytes of RAM per inode.
100M files = 15 GB NameNode RAM just for metadata
S3 LIST is expensive and paginated — listing 500K files takes seconds + API calls
Slow job startup even before any data is read
Spark creates one task per file (or per N bytes). Too many tasks = driver OOM + scheduling overhead
Each file has a Parquet footer read (round trip). 10K files = 10K metadata reads.
Minutes of overhead before first row is processed

sections.title: coalesce() vs repartition() at Write Time

blocks.content: The simplest fix: control the number of output files by adjusting partition count before writing.

# coalesce(N) — reduce partitions WITHOUT a full shuffle
# Works by combining adjacent partitions → fast but can create imbalanced files
df.coalesce(10).write.parquet('s3://bucket/output/')
# → 10 output files (may be unequal sizes)

# repartition(N) — full shuffle → balanced files
df.repartition(10).write.parquet('s3://bucket/output/')
# → 10 equally-sized files (but costs a shuffle)

# repartition by column — one file per partition value group
df.repartition('country').write \
  .partitionBy('country') \
  .parquet('s3://bucket/output/')
# → One file per distinct country (may still have many files per country partition)

# For final writes where file count matters more than sort order:
# coalesce is preferred (no shuffle cost)
# For skewed data that needs balancing: repartition

# Rule of thumb: aim for 128-512 MB files for Parquet
target_file_size_mb = 256
num_partitions = max(1, int(df_size_mb / target_file_size_mb))
df.coalesce(num_partitions).write.parquet('...')

Delta Lake's OPTIMIZE command performs bin-packing: it groups small files together and rewrites them as larger files up to the target file size (default 1 GB). Unlike coalesce/repartition, OPTIMIZE is deterministic, atomic, and runs in the background without blocking reads.

# Compact the entire table
spark.sql('OPTIMIZE my_delta_table')

# Compact only a specific partition (efficient — only touches changed data)
spark.sql("""
    OPTIMIZE my_delta_table
    WHERE dt = '2024-01-15'
""")

# Configure target file size (default 1 GB = 1073741824 bytes)
# For frequently-queried tables, 128-256 MB is often better
spark.conf.set('spark.databricks.delta.optimize.maxFileSize', 128 * 1024 * 1024)

# After OPTIMIZE, clean up old small files (they're still in _delta_log as 'removed')
# but still on disk — vacuum removes them
spark.sql('VACUUM my_delta_table RETAIN 168 HOURS')  # keep 7 days history
On Databricks, Delta tables can be configured to optimize automatically. This avoids the need for scheduled OPTIMIZE jobs.

# Enable auto-optimize on a table
spark.sql("""
    ALTER TABLE my_delta_table
    SET TBLPROPERTIES (
        'delta.autoOptimize.optimizeWrite' = 'true',   -- coalesce small writes before landing
        'delta.autoOptimize.autoCompact'   = 'true'    -- compact after each write
    )
""")

# optimizeWrite: batches the write into fewer, larger files before committing
# autoCompact: after each write, runs a background OPTIMIZE on recently-written files

# Check file sizes after compaction
spark.sql("""
    SELECT file_name, size_bytes
    FROM (DESCRIBE DETAIL my_delta_table)
""")

Interview answer: Small file problem → three solutions: (1) coalesce/repartition before writing, (2) Delta OPTIMIZE (bin-packing + optional ZORDER), (3) auto-optimize properties on the table. In prod, use all three in layers.

rune_cards.definition: Delta OPTIMIZE algorithm: groups small files into bins up to the target size and rewrites them as new larger files. Atomic — the transaction log records removed small files and new large files atomically. Old files remain until VACUUM.

Reduces partition count WITHOUT a full shuffle by combining adjacent partitions. Cheap — no network transfer. Produces N output files but potentially unbalanced sizes. Preferred over repartition() for final write control.

Delta command that physically deletes data files that are no longer referenced by the transaction log (old versions, files removed by OPTIMIZE). Default: retains 7 days (168 hours) for time travel. Safe to vacuum older than retention period.

Delta table property (delta.autoOptimize.optimizeWrite). When true, Spark uses a larger target file size when writing, automatically coalescing small output files before committing the transaction. Avoids creating small files in the first place.

quiz.question: A streaming job writes to a Delta table every 2 minutes, creating ~30 small files per write. After 1 week: 15,000 small files. Queries are slow at startup. What's the most operationally effective fix?
options: Schedule a nightly OPTIMIZE on completed date partitions
Use VACUUM every hour to delete old files
Reduce the streaming trigger interval to once per hour
quiz.feedback: Scheduled OPTIMIZE compacts small files via bin-packing and is atomic + non-blocking. VACUUM deletes old files but doesn't compact. Reducing trigger interval lowers throughput. shuffle.partitions affects shuffles not writes. OPTIMIZE nightly on closed partitions is the standard production pattern.

quiz.question: You have a batch job that produces a 100 GB output DataFrame. You want exactly 100 output Parquet files of equal size. Which is better: coalesce(100) or repartition(100)?
options: repartition(100) — full shuffle guarantees balanced 1 GB files
quiz.feedback: repartition(100) does a full hash-based shuffle distributing rows evenly across 100 partitions → 100 roughly equal 1 GB files. coalesce(100) just merges adjacent partitions without shuffling → may produce very unbalanced files if the upstream partitioning was skewed.
